questions.q: In a patient with Polycythemia Vera, what artifactual result is expected for PT and aPTT if the Hematocrit is > 55%?
questions.exp: A high haematocrit (Hct >55%) means there is proportionally less plasma, causing excess sodium citrate anticoagulant in the tube. The surplus citrate chelates the calcium added during testing, resulting in falsely prolonged PT and aPTT.

questions.q: To correct the pre-analytical error mentioned in the previous question, what action should be taken?
opts: A. Re-draw using an EDTA tube
B. Increase the volume of Sodium Citrate
C. Decrease the volume of Sodium Citrate according to a correction formula
questions.exp: The anticoagulant volume must be reduced using a correction formula: Vol = (100 − Hct) / (595 − Hct) × total volume.

questions.q: What is the typical difference in Fibrinogen levels between early sepsis and Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC)?
opts: B. Increased in Sepsis (acute phase reactant); Decreased in DIC (consumption)
C. Decreased in Sepsis; Increased in DIC
questions.exp: Fibrinogen rises as an acute-phase reactant in early sepsis, but falls in DIC due to widespread consumption in microvascular thrombi.

questions.q: A 10-year-old child presents with pancytopenia, MCV 104 fL, and rare blasts on the peripheral smear with no hypersegmented neutrophils. What is the most likely cause of the macrocytosis?
opts: C. Ineffective erythropoiesis due to marrow infiltration by blasts
questions.exp: Marrow infiltration by leukaemic blasts causes ineffective erythropoiesis, leading to macrocytosis.

questions.q: When differentiating Iron Deficiency Anemia (IDA) from Thalassemia, which parameter is typically significantly elevated in Thalassemia?
opts: C. RBC Count (relative to the Hb level)
questions.exp: In thalassaemia carriers, the RBC count is disproportionately high relative to the degree of anaemia and low Hb.

questions.q: In advanced liver disease, INR is prolonged and Factor V is decreased. In Vitamin K deficiency, what is the status of Factor V?
questions.exp: Factor V is liver-synthesised but not vitamin K-dependent; Factors II, VII, IX, and X are vitamin K-dependent.

questions.q: Regarding the genetic mutation in Sickle Cell Disease, which is correct?
opts: A. Substitution of glutamic acid with valine at position 6 of the β-globin chain
B. Substitution of glutamic acid with lysine at position 6 of the β-globin chain
C. Deletion of the α-globin gene
D. Substitution at position 26 of the β-globin chain
questions.exp: This is the classic sickle cell mutation: codon 6 of the β-globin chain changes glutamic acid (Glu) to valine (Val).

questions.q: A patient has Hb: 85 g/L, elevated LDH, decreased Haptoglobin, and elevated Unconjugated Bilirubin. Which test is most useful to confirm immune-mediated hemolysis?
questions.exp: The Direct Antiglobulin Test (DAT) confirms whether IgG or C3 is coating the red cell surface, establishing immune-mediated haemolysis.

questions.q: In flow cytometry for Paroxysmal Nocturnal Hemoglobinuria (PNH), which proteins are primarily missing from the cell surface?
questions.exp: PNH involves a GPI-anchor protein gene mutation, resulting in loss of CD55 and CD59 from the cell surface.

questions.q: What is the typical bone marrow finding in Aplastic Anemia?
opts: B. Hypocellularity with fat cells replacing hematopoietic cells (<25% cellularity)
questions.exp: Aplastic Anaemia marrow appears 'empty' — haematopoietic cells are markedly reduced and replaced by fat cells (cellularity <25%).

questions.q: A 65-year-old male on Warfarin has an INR of 6.5 and active bleeding. What should be administered in addition to stopping the drug?
opts: B. Prothrombin Complex Concentrate (PCC) and Vitamin K
questions.exp: PCC (e.g. Beriplex/Octaplex) provides immediate clotting factor replacement for haemostasis; Vitamin K is co-administered for sustained reversal.

questions.q: Which clotting factors are the most labile in plasma stored at 4°C?
questions.exp: Factors V and VIII are the most labile at 4°C, losing activity rapidly during storage.

questions.q: A patient with severe anemia has a reticulocyte count of 3% and Hb of 70 g/L. How is the corrected Reticulocyte Index (RI) interpreted?
questions.exp: When severe anaemia is present, an RI <2% indicates inadequate bone marrow compensatory response.

questions.q: In a Lupus Anticoagulant test, what does a shortening of the aPTT after adding excess phospholipid indicate?
opts: B. Presence of a phospholipid-dependent inhibitor
questions.exp: Lupus anticoagulant is a phospholipid-dependent inhibitor; adding excess phospholipid neutralises it, shortening the aPTT.

questions.q: Peripheral smear findings in Myelofibrosis typically include:
opts: A. Teardrop cells (Dacrocytes) and leucoerythroblastic features
questions.exp: Classic peripheral blood findings in myelofibrosis are teardrop cells (dacrocytes) and a leucoerythroblastic picture.

questions.q: During an acute hemolytic episode in G6PD deficiency, what is most commonly observed on the peripheral smear?
opts: B. Bite cells and Heinz bodies (with supravital stain)
questions.exp: In a G6PD deficiency haemolytic episode, denatured haemoglobin forms Heinz bodies (supravital stain required); the spleen 'bites' them out, leaving bite cells.

questions.q: In Australian laboratories, D-dimer is primarily used to exclude which of the following?
opts: B. Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) and Pulmonary Embolism (PE)
questions.exp: D-dimer has a high negative predictive value for excluding DVT and PE.

questions.q: The presence of significant Schistocytes and thrombocytopenia on a peripheral smear should lead to strong suspicion of:
questions.exp: Significant schistocytes plus thrombocytopenia is the hallmark of microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia (MAHA), strongly suggesting TTP or HUS.

questions.q: What is the most common cause of a prolonged Thrombin Time (TT)?
questions.exp: Thrombin Time measures fibrinogen-to-fibrin conversion and is highly sensitive to heparin interference and hypofibrinogenaemia.

questions.q: Which is true regarding Howell-Jolly bodies?
opts: B. They are remnants of nuclear DNA, commonly seen post-splenectomy
questions.exp: Howell-Jolly bodies are nuclear DNA remnants normally cleared by the spleen; they appear abundantly post-splenectomy.

questions.q: A 25-year-old female has Hb 105 g/L, MCV 72 fL, and RDW 18%. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.exp: IDA is characterised by a markedly elevated RDW (anisocytosis), whereas thalassaemia trait typically shows a normal RDW.

questions.q: Typical coagulation results for Hemophilia A are:
questions.exp: Haemophilia A lacks Factor VIII, an intrinsic pathway factor, giving a prolonged aPTT with a normal PT.

questions.q: In testing for Von Willebrand Disease (vWD), which activity must be measured alongside vWF Antigen?
questions.exp: The Ristocetin Cofactor Activity (vWF:RCo) assay measures the functional activity of vWF in binding platelets.

questions.q: In flow cytometry, CD34 positivity indicates which type of cell?
questions.exp: CD34 is a specific surface marker of early haematopoietic stem/progenitor cells.

questions.q: What are the nuclear characteristics of neutrophils in Pelger-Huet anomaly?
questions.exp: Pelger-Huet anomaly causes hyposegmentation of neutrophils, typically presenting as bilobed (pince-nez) or non-segmented nuclei.

questions.q: Which test distinguishes a Leukemoid Reaction from Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML)?
questions.exp: The LAP score is markedly elevated in leukaemoid reactions (infection) and markedly low in CML.

questions.q: Platelet aggregation studies show no response to ADP, Collagen, or Epinephrine, but a normal response to Ristocetin. This is characteristic of:
questions.exp: Glanzmann Thrombasthenia lacks GPIIb/IIIa, abolishing aggregation responses to ADP, collagen, and epinephrine; the Ristocetin pathway (vWF/GPIb) remains intact.

questions.q: What is the gold standard for Malaria diagnosis?
opts: B. Examination of Thick and Thin blood films
questions.exp: Thick and thin blood film microscopy is the gold standard for diagnosing malaria, identifying species, and quantifying parasite load.

questions.q: What is the specimen requirement for ESR (Erythrocyte Sedimentation Rate) in Australian labs?
opts: A. EDTA tube, tested within 4 hours
questions.exp: Australian ESR standard uses a black-top citrate tube at a 1:4 ratio (1 part anticoagulant to 4 parts blood).

questions.q: Peripheral smear characteristics of Megaloblastic Anemia include:
opts: A. Target cells and basophilic stippling
questions.exp: Megaloblastic anaemia from impaired DNA synthesis shows macro-ovalocytes and hypersegmented neutrophils (>5 lobes) on the peripheral smear.

questions.q: In Liver Function Tests (LFTs), if ALP and GGT are significantly elevated while ALT/AST are only mildly increased, what is the most likely pattern?
questions.exp: A pattern of markedly elevated ALP and GGT with only mildly raised ALT/AST indicates cholestatic disease.

questions.q: If BUN is significantly elevated but Creatinine is normal, which non-renal factor is a likely cause?
opts: B. High protein diet or GI bleed
questions.exp: A high-protein diet or GI bleed increases nitrogenous waste, causing a pre-renal rise in BUN without a proportional rise in creatinine.

questions.q: A 58-year-old male with a mechanical valve, INR 6.2, presents with flank pain and hematuria. CT shows a wedge-shaped renal lesion. Most likely diagnosis?
opts: B. Renal infarction due to embolism
questions.exp: Over-anticoagulation with warfarin (INR 6.2) can cause warfarin-induced nephropathy via direct tubular injury presenting with acute haematuria.

questions.q: In electrolyte analysis, which group of analytes will be falsely elevated if the sample is hemolyzed?
questions.exp: K⁺, Mg²⁺, and LDH are concentrated inside RBCs; haemolysis releases them into the sample, causing falsely elevated results.

questions.q: HbA1c primarily reflects the average blood glucose over what period?
questions.exp: HbA1c reflects the average blood glucose over the RBC lifespan (~120 days), representing approximately the past 8–12 weeks.

questions.q: The primary mechanism for neurological complications in Hyponatremia is:
questions.exp: Hyponatraemia lowers extracellular osmolality, driving water into brain cells and causing cerebral oedema.

questions.q: What is the typical ECG change in a patient with severe Hyperkalemia?
questions.exp: Peaked T-waves are the earliest and most classic ECG sign of hyperkalaemic cardiac toxicity.

questions.q: Which factor is NOT typically used in the Australian eGFR calculation (e.g., CKD-EPI)?
questions.exp: The CKD-EPI eGFR formula uses only serum creatinine, age, and sex; body weight is not included.

questions.q: Lipemic samples primarily interfere with which type of detection?
questions.exp: Lipaemia causes turbidity that scatters light, significantly interfering with photometric (absorbance-based) assays.

questions.q: What is the typical compensation for Metabolic Acidosis?
questions.exp: In metabolic acidosis, the respiratory system compensates by hyperventilating to lower pCO₂ and raise blood pH.

questions.q: A 40-year-old female presents with fatigue and cold intolerance. Results: High TSH, Low Free T4. Most likely diagnosis?
questions.exp: High TSH with low Free T4 indicates primary hypothyroidism; Hashimoto's Thyroiditis is the most common underlying cause.

questions.q: If blood is not separated promptly, glucose levels decrease at what rate per hour?
questions.exp: If blood is not promptly separated, in-vitro glycolysis by blood cells reduces glucose by approximately 5–7% per hour.

questions.q: The Jaffe reaction is used to measure:
questions.exp: The Jaffe reaction quantifies creatinine using an alkaline picric acid solution that produces a colour change.

questions.q: Which cardiac marker rises fastest and is most specific after Acute Myocardial Infarction (AMI)?
questions.exp: High-sensitivity Troponin (hs-Troponin) is the most cardiac-specific marker and rises rapidly after AMI, remaining elevated for days.

questions.q: What is a common cause of elevated Unconjugated Bilirubin?
questions.exp: Haemolytic disease causes massive RBC destruction, releasing unconjugated bilirubin that overwhelms hepatic conjugation capacity.

questions.q: An elevated Anion Gap is commonly seen in:
questions.exp: Ketoacidosis accumulates unmeasured acids (e.g. acetoacetate), raising the anion gap.

questions.q: Which substance clearance is most commonly used to estimate renal function?
questions.exp: Endogenous creatinine clearance is the most widely used clinical method to estimate GFR.

questions.q: To avoid Kernicterus in neonatal jaundice, which indicator must be monitored?
opts: B. Total Bilirubin and Free Bilirubin
questions.exp: In neonatal jaundice, free unconjugated bilirubin can cross the blood-brain barrier causing kernicterus; total and free bilirubin must be monitored closely.

questions.q: Bence-Jones proteins are primarily found in the:
questions.exp: Bence-Jones proteins are free immunoglobulin light chains; their small molecular weight allows glomerular filtration, so they appear primarily in urine.

questions.q: Serum osmolality primarily reflects the concentration of:
questions.exp: Serum osmolality is primarily determined by small solutes: sodium (and its paired anions), glucose, and urea. Calculated as: 2[Na] + Glucose + Urea.

questions.q: Typical CSF findings in Bacterial Meningitis include:
opts: B. Increased WBC (Neutrophils), Decreased Glucose, Increased Protein
questions.exp: Bacterial meningitis CSF shows markedly raised WBC (predominantly neutrophils), low glucose (consumed by bacteria), and high protein (inflammatory permeability).

questions.q: What is the typical IMViC reaction sequence for E. coli?
questions.exp: E. coli has a classic IMViC pattern of ++-- (Indole positive, MR positive, VP negative, Citrate negative).

questions.q: In a Gram Stain, Gram-negative bacteria appear pink/red because:
opts: A. They have a thick peptidoglycan layer
B. They lose crystal violet and take up the Safranin counterstain
C. They have an outer membrane
questions.exp: Gram-negative bacteria have a thin peptidoglycan layer; during decolourisation with alcohol, crystal violet is lost and the safranin counterstain is taken up, resulting in a pink/red appearance.

questions.q: Which antibody is the only one capable of crossing the placenta?
questions.exp: IgG is the only immunoglobulin actively transported across the placenta via the neonatal Fc receptor (FcRn).

questions.q: Detection of elevated IgM in a newborn typically indicates:
questions.exp: Maternal IgM is too large to cross the placenta; elevated IgM in a newborn must be self-produced in response to intrauterine infection.

questions.q: The Catalase test is used to differentiate between:
questions.exp: The catalase test is the fundamental test distinguishing Staphylococcus (positive — bubbles) from Streptococcus (negative).

questions.q: Pink colonies on MacConkey Agar indicate that the bacteria:
questions.exp: Pink colonies on MacConkey Agar indicate lactose fermentation, producing acid that changes the pH indicator colour.

questions.q: What is the hemolysis pattern of Streptococcus pneumoniae on Blood Agar?
questions.exp: Streptococcus pneumoniae produces alpha-haemolysis (incomplete haemolysis, green zone) on blood agar.

questions.q: The best medium for culturing Neisseria gonorrhoeae is:
questions.exp: Neisseria gonorrhoeae is fastidious and requires nutrient-rich chocolate agar or selective Thayer-Martin medium for growth.

questions.q: Which bacterium produces a 'metallic green sheen' on EMB Agar?
questions.exp: E. coli ferments lactose intensely; the resulting acid causes dye precipitation on EMB Agar, producing the characteristic metallic green sheen.

questions.q: The key biochemical test to identify Staphylococcus aureus is:
questions.exp: The coagulase test is the gold-standard key test distinguishing S. aureus (coagulase-positive) from coagulase-negative staphylococci (CoNS).

questions.q: In antibiotic sensitivity testing (AST), the E-test is used to measure:
questions.exp: The E-test strip carries a continuous antibiotic gradient, allowing direct reading of the Minimum Inhibitory Concentration (MIC).

questions.q: What does the VDRL/RPR screening test for Syphilis detect?
questions.exp: VDRL/RPR are non-treponemal tests that detect non-specific reagin (anti-cardiolipin antibody) produced in response to tissue damage from syphilis.

questions.q: Which immune cell plays a primary role in fighting parasitic infections?
questions.exp: Eosinophils contain major basic protein and play a primary defensive role against parasitic (helminth) infections and allergic reactions.

questions.q: The classical complement pathway is activated by:
questions.exp: The classical complement pathway is activated by antigen-antibody complexes (IgG or IgM) binding C1.

questions.q: A 'Speckled pattern' in an ANA test is commonly associated with:
questions.exp: A speckled ANA pattern is associated with antibodies against extractable nuclear antigens (ENA), commonly seen in MCTD or Sjögren's Syndrome.

questions.q: An elevated Anti-Streptolysin O (ASO) titer indicates:
opts: B. Recent Group A Streptococcus infection
questions.exp: A raised ASO titre indicates recent Group A Streptococcal infection.

questions.q: The standard stain for screening Mycobacterium tuberculosis (MTB) is:
questions.exp: MTB has a wax-rich cell wall (mycolic acids) that resists Gram staining; Ziehl-Neelsen (acid-fast) stain is required.

questions.q: Which virus is strongly associated with cervical cancer?
questions.exp: HPV, particularly high-risk strains (HPV 16 and 18), is strongly causally associated with cervical cancer.

questions.q: The primary advantage of ELISA technology in antigen detection is:
questions.exp: ELISA's key advantage is its high sensitivity through enzymatic signal amplification and its ability to provide accurate quantitative results.

questions.q: For crossmatching infants < 3 months old, which sample is preferred for antibody screening?
questions.exp: Infants <3 months rarely produce clinically significant antibodies; any antibodies present are likely maternal IgG transferred transplacentally, so maternal serum is preferred for antibody screening.

questions.q: A patient with Bombay Phenotype (hh) is likely to be misidentified as which blood group in routine typing?
questions.exp: Bombay phenotype (hh) lacks H substance; forward typing shows no reactivity with Anti-A/Anti-B, making it appear identical to Group O.

questions.q: In the MNS blood group system, which antibody is most commonly associated with Hemolytic Disease of the Newborn (HDN)?
questions.exp: Anti-S and Anti-s in the MNS system have greater clinical significance than Anti-M/N and are more commonly associated with HDN.

questions.q: What are the requirements for blood used for infants < 3 months old?
questions.exp: Neonatal transfusions require: CMV-negative or leucodepleted blood (prevent viral transmission), irradiation (prevent GvHD), and fresh blood (≤5–7 days, to avoid hyperkalaemia).

questions.q: A pregnant woman has a positive antibody screen (Anti-D, titer 1:4) after receiving RhIG. This is likely:
questions.exp: After RhIG injection, low-titre (e.g. 1:4) Anti-D detected in maternal serum is passive antibody that persists for several weeks.

questions.q: The most common cause of Acute Hemolytic Transfusion Reactions (AHTR) is:
opts: B. Patient identification or clerical errors
D. Allergic reaction to plasma proteins
questions.exp: The most common root cause of acute haemolytic transfusion reactions is not a missed antibody on screening but rather administrative or patient identification errors.

questions.q: A patient develops jaundice, falling Hb, and a positive DAT 7 days after transfusion. This is likely:
questions.exp: Jaundice, falling Hb, and a positive DAT appearing days post-transfusion is the classic presentation of a Delayed Haemolytic Transfusion Reaction (DHTR), caused by an anamnestic immune response.

questions.q: Transfusion Related Acute Lung Injury (TRALI) is characterized by non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema within how many hours of transfusion?
questions.exp: TRALI is defined as non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema occurring within 6 hours of transfusion, typically associated with donor HLA antibodies.

questions.q: How is Transfusion Associated Circulatory Overload (TACO) differentiated from TRALI?
opts: A. TACO presents with hypertension and responds to diuretics
questions.exp: TACO is due to volume overload (hydrostatic pressure); it presents with hypertension and responds to diuretics. TRALI is inflammatory (increased permeability) and does not respond to diuretics.

questions.q: What is the expiry of Deglycerolized RBCs stored at 4°C after wash in an open system?
questions.exp: After thawing and washing in an open system, deglycerolised RBCs must be used within 24 hours due to the risk of bacterial contamination from breaking the sterile seal.

questions.q: What are the storage conditions for Platelet Concentrates?
questions.exp: Platelet concentrates must be stored at 20–24°C with constant agitation to prevent spontaneous aggregation and maintain appropriate pH.

questions.q: Pre-storage Leukoreduction does NOT aim to:
questions.exp: Pre-storage leukoreduction aims to reduce FNHTR, prevent CMV transmission, and prevent HLA alloimmunisation; it does not directly extend the physical lifespan of RBCs.

questions.q: In an emergency with no time for crossmatch, what blood should be issued?
opts: C. Group O Rh Negative (females/children) or O Rh Positive (adult males)
questions.exp: In an emergency without time for crossmatch: O Rh Negative blood is given to females of childbearing potential and children (to avoid anti-D immunisation); O Rh Positive is acceptable for adult males.

questions.q: Once thawed, FFP should be stored at 2-6°C and used within how many hours?
questions.exp: Once thawed, FFP must be used within 24 hours at 2–6°C because labile clotting factors (V and VIII) degrade rapidly.

questions.q: What are the primary components of Cryoprecipitate?
opts: A. Factor VIII, Fibrinogen, vWF, Factor XIII
C. Factors II, VII, IX, X
questions.exp: Cryoprecipitate is rich in fibrinogen, Factor VIII, vWF, and Factor XIII, making it essential in major haemorrhage management.

questions.q: What is the function of a Mordant in histological staining?
opts: B. To act as a nuclear counterstain
C. To form a dye-tissue complex to fix the color
questions.exp: A mordant acts as a bridge, forming a stable dye-tissue chelate complex that fixes the colour to the tissue.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a mordant?
questions.exp: Xylene is not a mordant; it is used in histological processing as a clearing agent and dewaxing solvent.

questions.q: Why is EDTA the preferred anticoagulant for genomic studies?
opts: B. It chelates metal ions, inhibiting metal-dependent DNases
C. It is cheaper than Heparin
questions.exp: EDTA chelates metal ions (e.g. Mg²⁺), inhibiting metal-dependent DNases and thus protecting DNA from enzymatic degradation — making it ideal for genomic studies.

questions.q: Why is Heparin NOT recommended for PCR testing?
opts: B. It inhibits the PCR amplification reaction
D. It does not prevent clotting
questions.exp: Heparin binds to and inhibits DNA polymerase, strongly suppressing the PCR amplification reaction.

questions.q: In molecular biology, EDTA chelating calcium ions prevents clotting and also prevents:
questions.exp: By chelating divalent calcium and magnesium ions, EDTA inactivates many metal-dependent nucleases, preventing enzymatic DNA degradation.

questions.q: What is the principle of Formalin fixation?
questions.exp: Formalin fixation works by forming methylene bridges between proteins (cross-linking), preserving tissue architecture.

questions.q: What is the standard thickness for paraffin sections?
questions.exp: The standard thickness for paraffin sections for routine light microscopy is 3–5 μm, allowing visualisation of single-cell layers.

questions.q: The primary difference between Quality Assurance (QA) and Quality Control (QC) is:
opts: A. QA is retrospective; QC is prospective
B. QA focuses on process and prevention; QC focuses on output and error detection
C. QA is for chemistry only; QC is for all
questions.exp: QA focuses on the overall process and error prevention (system-level); QC focuses on monitoring test outputs and detecting errors (result-level).

questions.q: Which is correct regarding needle handling?
opts: B. Dispose in yellow sharps container; NEVER recap
D. Disinfect with alcohol before disposal
questions.exp: Needles must never be recapped after use — recapping is the leading cause of needlestick injuries. Dispose immediately in a yellow sharps container.

questions.q: What does a 1:3s Westgard violation mean?
opts: A. A single control value exceeds the mean by 3 SD; reject the run
B. Two consecutive values exceed 2 SD
questions.exp: A 1:3s Westgard violation means a single QC value exceeds the mean ±3 SD, indicating major random or systematic error; the run must be rejected.

questions.q: Australian standards (NATA/RCPA) require that Critical Results be:
opts: B. Immediately phoned to the clinician and documented
D. Logged in the system only
questions.exp: Australian NATA/RCPA guidelines require critical results to be phoned immediately to the clinician and fully documented in the LIS.

questions.q: If a needle-stick injury occurs, what is the first step?
opts: B. Wash the wound under running water and encourage bleeding
C. Ask the patient for their history
D. Call a meeting with the manager
questions.exp: The immediate first step after a needlestick is to wash the wound under running water and gently encourage bleeding to flush out contaminants.

questions.q: What is the chemical safety documentation in a lab called?
questions.exp: The Safety Data Sheet (SDS) is the mandatory documentation for chemical safety in laboratories, detailing hazards, properties, and emergency procedures.

questions.q: Standard Deviation (SD) measures the:
questions.exp: Standard deviation (SD) measures the dispersion of data around the mean, used in laboratory statistics to assess measurement precision (reproducibility).

questions.q: What is the most common pre-analytical error in the laboratory?
questions.exp: Despite advances in automation, patient/specimen mislabelling remains the most common pre-analytical error in the clinical laboratory cycle.
